class_schedule.topic: Level Design Principles and Player Flow
learning_objectives: Identify the core principles of effective level design
Analyze player flow through spatial layouts using heat maps and critical paths
Distinguish between designer intent and emergent player behavior
readings: An Architectural Approach to Level Design Ch. 1-2 (Christopher Totten)
Level Up! The Guide to Great Video Game Design Ch. 10 (Scott Rogers)

lecture_notes.introduction: Level design is the invisible hand guiding the player through an experience. Great level design feels natural, but every sight line, chokepoint, and reward placement is a deliberate choice. This session establishes the vocabulary and principles we will use all semester.
key_concepts: The teaching triad: introduce a mechanic safely, test it in controlled risk, challenge it under pressure
Weenies and landmarks: visual attractors that orient the player without UI markers
Critical path vs exploration space: the required route versus optional reward areas
Flow theory applied to spatial design: balancing challenge and skill through level geometry
The 30-second loop: how a single room or arena should deliver a complete micro-experience
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing the first level of Super Mario Bros. (World 1-1) to identify the teaching triad, visual language, and critical path design
setup_requirements: Unreal Engine 5.3 installed with starter content
Graph paper or digital diagramming tool for top-down maps

review.value: Introduce: show the mechanic in a safe context. Test: require using it with some risk. Challenge: demand mastery under pressure. This three-beat rhythm appears in virtually every well-designed level.
A visually prominent landmark visible from the player's current position that communicates 'go there' without explicit UI — coined by Walt Disney for Disneyland's castle visible from every land

learning_objectives: Map emotional intensity curves for existing game levels
Design pacing structures that alternate tension and relief
Apply the concept of narrative beats to spatial progression
readings: An Architectural Approach to Level Design Ch. 4 (Christopher Totten)

lecture_notes.introduction: A level that is always intense is exhausting. A level that is always calm is boring. Great pacing alternates between peaks of intensity and valleys of rest, and the shape of that alternation defines the emotional signature of the experience.
key_concepts: Intensity curves: plotting combat density, puzzle difficulty, and narrative tension over time
The sawtooth pattern: gradual tension buildup followed by sharp release (boss rooms, safe rooms)
Breathing rooms: spaces that exist purely to let the player regroup, save, and absorb what happened
Environmental storytelling as pacing tool: narrative moments that slow gameplay without cutscenes
Tempo manipulation through geometry: long corridors build anticipation, open arenas signal combat
lecture_notes.demonstration: Mapping the intensity curve of Resident Evil 4's village encounter, identifying how geometry, enemy spawn timing, and audio work together to create a pacing masterclass
setup_requirements: Intensity curve template (provided in course materials)
Reference videos of analyzed game segments

review.value: Tension rises gradually through escalating encounters, then drops sharply at resolution points (boss defeat, safe room). The next sawtooth starts from a slightly higher baseline, creating an overall ascending arc.
A deliberate low-intensity space (merchant area, vista overlook, save point) placed after high-intensity sequences. Without breathing rooms, players experience fatigue and disengage.

learning_objectives: Design environmental puzzles that use spatial reasoning and level mechanics
Create combat arenas with tactical depth through cover placement, elevation, and flanking routes
Balance puzzle and combat spaces to serve different player skill types
readings: Halo 2 Multiplayer Map Design GDC Talk (Jaime Griesemer)

lecture_notes.introduction: Puzzles and combat are the two primary activity types in most game levels. Each has its own spatial requirements, and the best levels weave both together so that solving the puzzle changes the combat space, or winning the fight reveals the solution.
key_concepts: Lock-and-key design: gates that require specific items, abilities, or actions to pass
Spatial puzzles: platforming sequences, pressure plates, line-of-sight triggers, physics objects
Combat arena anatomy: entry points, cover positions, flanking routes, high ground, kill zones
The combat bowl: a depression or enclosed area that channels players toward the center while enemies control the rim
Integrated design: puzzles that modify the combat arena (raising bridges, draining water, activating turrets)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Grayboxing a two-room sequence in Unreal Engine: a puzzle room where activating switches reconfigures the adjacent combat arena's cover layout
setup_requirements: Unreal Engine 5.3 with BSP brush tools
Basic understanding of Blueprint visual scripting for triggers

review.value: An arena shape where the play area is lower than the surrounding terrain. Enemies spawn or position on the elevated rim, creating natural cover-to-cover gameplay as the player pushes uphill. Used extensively in Halo and Destiny.

A progression gate where the 'lock' is any obstacle and the 'key' is any capability that overcomes it. Keys can be items (keycards), abilities (double jump), knowledge (a password), or environmental changes (draining water).

learning_objectives: Compare the design constraints of open-world and linear level structures
Design hub-and-spoke layouts that offer player choice within a structured framework
Understand how open-world games manage pacing without linear control
readings: An Architectural Approach to Level Design Ch. 7 (Christopher Totten)
GDC 2017: 'Building Non-Linear Narratives in Horizon Zero Dawn' (John Gonzalez)

lecture_notes.introduction: Linear design gives the designer near-total control over pacing and information delivery. Open-world design trades that control for player agency. Most shipped games exist on a spectrum between these extremes, and understanding the tradeoffs lets you choose the right structure for your game's goals.
key_concepts: Linear corridors: maximum pacing control, predictable difficulty curves, scripted set-pieces
Hub-and-spoke: a central area connecting to self-contained levels, combining exploration freedom with level-scale pacing control
Open world: player-driven exploration, points of interest as content anchors, scaling difficulty by region
Gating in open worlds: soft gates (enemy difficulty), hard gates (story locks), natural gates (terrain)
Content density: the amount of interactive content per unit of traversal time, and how too little or too much damages the experience
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a hub-and-spoke layout on paper: a town hub connecting to a forest dungeon (linear), a mountain pass (semi-linear with optional caves), and a lake region (open mini-zone). Then grayboxing the hub in Unreal.

review.value: A central safe zone (the hub) with multiple exits leading to self-contained levels (spokes). The player chooses which spoke to tackle, but each spoke is internally linear. Used by Dark Souls bonfires, Metroid stations, and Mario 64's castle.

The ratio of meaningful interactions to traversal time. Ubisoft-style open worlds often suffer from icon fatigue (too high), while some survival games suffer from empty walking (too low). The sweet spot depends on the game's pace target.

learning_objectives: Implement basic procedural level generation algorithms
Understand the grayboxing workflow for rapid level prototyping
Evaluate when procedural generation enhances or harms level design quality
readings: Procedural Generation in Game Design Ch. 3-5 (Tanya X. Short & Tarn Adams)
Spelunky by Derek Yu, Boss Fight Books (Ch. 4-6)

lecture_notes.introduction: Procedural generation promises infinite content. The reality is more nuanced: algorithms can produce quantity, but quality requires constraints, templates, and careful validation. Today we study when and how to use procedural generation as a level design tool rather than a replacement for level design.
key_concepts: Binary space partitioning (BSP) for dungeon room generation: recursively subdivide a rectangle and connect rooms
Wave Function Collapse (WFC): constraint-based tile placement for coherent layouts
Template-based generation: hand-designed room chunks assembled by algorithm with connectivity rules
Grayboxing workflow: block out spaces with primitive geometry, playtest, iterate, then art pass
Validation passes: algorithmic checks that every generated level is completable (reachability, key-before-lock)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implementing a BSP dungeon generator in Python that outputs room rectangles and corridor connections, then importing the result into Unreal as a graybox

review.value: Recursively split a bounding rectangle along random horizontal or vertical lines until leaf nodes are small enough to be rooms. Connect sibling leaves with corridors. Guarantees full connectivity by construction.

Blocking out a level using untextured primitive geometry (boxes, ramps, cylinders) to test spatial flow, pacing, and gameplay before investing in art assets. A graybox should be playtested at least 3 times before the art pass begins.

learning_objectives: Conduct structured playtesting sessions that produce actionable feedback
Analyze playtest data including heat maps, completion rates, and player deaths
Iterate on level designs based on observed player behavior rather than designer assumptions

lecture_notes.introduction: The map in your head is not the map the player experiences. Playtesting is where designer intent collides with player reality. Every level design should be treated as a hypothesis that playtesting either validates or disproves.
key_concepts: Structured playtesting protocol: silent observation, think-aloud, post-session questionnaire
Heat map analysis: tracking player position, death locations, and time-spent to visualize flow problems
Completion funnel: percentage of players reaching each milestone, identifying where and why they drop off
The five-playtest rule: a level is not shippable until five independent playtesters can complete it without designer intervention
Iteration taxonomy: tuning (number changes), restructuring (layout changes), and rethinking (concept changes)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Running a live playtest of a student's graybox level: collecting heat map data via Unreal's analytics blueprint, then analyzing the results to identify a stuck point and iterating on the layout in real-time
setup_requirements: Playtestable graybox level from previous sessions
Analytics blueprint for position tracking (provided in course materials)
Screen recording software for think-aloud capture

review.value: If 100% of playtesters enter room 1 but only 40% reach room 3, there is a design problem between rooms 2 and 3. The funnel pinpoints exactly where to focus iteration effort.

Watching a playtester without offering any help or hints. The designer's instinct to say 'try looking left' is exactly the information the level should be communicating through its geometry and lighting.

quizzes.topic: Level Design Principles, Pacing, and Spatial Design

questions.question: A playtest reveals that 80% of players in your level run straight past an optional reward cave because they never notice its entrance. The cave entrance is on the left wall of a corridor where the critical path leads right and forward. Using level design principles from this course, describe two specific spatial modifications you would make to draw players toward the cave without placing a UI marker on it.
questions.answer: Modification 1: Place a weenie visible through the cave entrance — a glowing object, unique color, or light source that contrasts with the corridor's palette, creating a visual attractor that catches the player's peripheral vision. Modification 2: Bend the corridor so the player's forward path briefly faces the cave entrance before curving right. This forces the cave into the player's central vision for 1-2 seconds of travel, creating awareness without forcing a detour. Additionally, audio cues (water dripping, wind, distinct music) could reinforce the visual pull.

rubric.key: Proposes a visual attractor (weenie/lighting/contrast) at the entrance
Proposes a geometry change that puts the cave in the player's sightline
Explains why the modifications work in terms of player attention and flow

questions.question: Draw or describe an intensity curve for a 10-minute level segment that contains: a puzzle room, a combat arena, a breathing room, and a boss encounter. Label each section on the curve and explain why you ordered them the way you did.
questions.answer: The curve should follow a sawtooth pattern: (1) Puzzle room at medium intensity (2-4 minutes) — cognitively engaging but not physically threatening. (2) Combat arena at high intensity (2-3 minutes) — the puzzle solution opens the arena, and the difficulty spike rewards the mental effort with action. (3) Breathing room at low intensity (1-2 minutes) — a safe space with a save point, ammo refill, and environmental storytelling that contextualizes the upcoming boss. (4) Boss encounter at peak intensity (3-4 minutes) — the climax, drawing on skills from both the puzzle and combat sections. The ordering works because it follows the sawtooth buildup-release pattern twice: puzzle builds to combat (first release), breathing room builds to boss (second and larger release). Starting with the puzzle instead of combat eases the player in cognitively before demanding reflexive action.

rubric.key: Correct sawtooth shape with ascending intensity peaks
Each section is labeled with appropriate intensity level and duration
Justifies ordering with pacing principles (tension/release, cognitive before physical)

quizzes.topic: Open World Design, Procedural Generation, and Playtesting

questions.question: Compare the BSP dungeon generation algorithm with the Wave Function Collapse (WFC) algorithm. For each, describe in one sentence how it generates a layout, then explain one specific scenario where each algorithm is the better choice.
questions.answer: BSP recursively subdivides a bounding rectangle into leaf nodes that become rooms, then connects sibling nodes with corridors, guaranteeing full connectivity. WFC places tiles one at a time from a set of hand-authored examples, collapsing each tile's possibilities based on adjacency constraints propagated from already-placed neighbors. BSP is better for traditional dungeon-crawlers (Binding of Isaac, roguelikes) where rectangular rooms with corridor connections are the expected layout style and guaranteed connectivity is critical. WFC is better for organic or stylistically specific environments (town layouts, cave systems, overworld regions) where the output must match the aesthetic of hand-crafted examples and tile-level visual coherence matters more than geometric regularity.

rubric.key: Correct one-sentence description of BSP algorithm
Correct one-sentence description of WFC algorithm
Appropriate scenario where BSP is better with valid reasoning
Appropriate scenario where WFC is better with valid reasoning

questions.question: You run a playtest and observe the following data from 10 players: Room 1 (100% reach), Room 2 (100%), Room 3 (50%), Room 4 (50%), Boss Room (30%). Identify the most critical design problem, explain what type of playtesting data you would collect next to diagnose it, and propose a specific hypothesis for what might be causing it.
questions.answer: The critical problem is the 50% drop between Room 2 and Room 3 — half the players cannot or choose not to progress past that point. The secondary drop from Room 4 to the Boss Room (50% to 30%) is also concerning but smaller in absolute terms. Next data to collect: death heat map in Room 2/Room 3 transition, time-spent data for the 50% who did not reach Room 3, and think-aloud recordings to determine if players are stuck (ability gate), dying (difficulty spike), or lost (navigation failure). Hypothesis: The transition from Room 2 to Room 3 contains an ability gate (e.g., a jump gap or puzzle mechanic) that was not adequately taught in Rooms 1-2, so players who happen to discover the mechanic progress while others do not. This is consistent with a binary pass/fail drop rather than a gradual decline.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies Room 2-to-3 as the primary drop point
Specifies appropriate data to collect (heat maps, time-spent, think-aloud)
Proposes a specific, testable hypothesis for the cause

major_assignments.title: Complete Level Design: From Concept to Playtested Graybox
major_assignments.description: Design, graybox, and playtest a complete single-player game level in Unreal Engine 5. The level must be 8-12 minutes of gameplay, include at least one puzzle sequence, one combat arena, and one exploration/reward area. You must conduct at least two formal playtesting sessions with different participants and iterate on the design based on observed data. Submit both the original and iterated graybox to demonstrate how playtesting changed your design.
objectives: Apply the full level design pipeline from concept document through grayboxing to playtested iteration
Demonstrate mastery of pacing, player flow, and spatial composition
Use playtesting data to make evidence-based design decisions
Produce a level that communicates its critical path and rewards exploration without relying on UI markers
deliverables: Level design document (concept, floor plan, intensity curve, target experience)
Unreal Engine project with both original and iterated graybox versions
Playtesting report with heat maps, completion funnel data, and player feedback summaries
Post-mortem reflection (1-2 pages) analyzing what changed and why

criteria: Level design document clearly articulates the target experience
Floor plan demonstrates understanding of flow, pacing, and spatial composition
Intensity curve matches the intended emotional journey
Puzzle, combat, and exploration areas serve distinct roles in the pacing
Level is navigable and completable without external guidance
Spatial composition uses weenies, sight lines, and lighting to guide the player
Scale and proportions feel appropriate for the gameplay style
Combat arenas have tactical depth with cover, elevation, and flanking options
At least two formal playtests conducted with documented protocol
Heat map and completion funnel data collected and analyzed
Specific design problems identified from observed player behavior
Iteration decisions are justified by playtesting evidence, not designer preference
Iterated graybox shows measurable improvement in completion rates or flow
Changes address the specific problems identified in playtesting
Post-mortem demonstrates honest self-assessment and design growth
Exploration rewards are discoverable by at least 50% of playtesters
Complete level design pipeline from concept through playtested iteration
Effective use of pacing, flow, and spatial composition principles
Evidence-based iteration driven by playtesting data
Professional documentation and honest self-assessment

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes assessing understanding of level design theory, spatial design principles, and analysis skills

grayboxing_exercises.description: Weekly in-class grayboxing exercises building specific spatial design skills

major_assignment.description: Full level design project from concept through playtested graybox iteration

level_analysis_papers.description: Two written analyses of published game levels applying course concepts to real-world examples

final_portfolio.description: Curated portfolio of all graybox work, playtesting reports, and a final polished level design

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all playtesting sessions and critiques. Missing a playtesting session without prior arrangement will result in a zero for the associated exercise, as playtesting requires participant presence.
course_policies.late_submissions: Design documents and written assignments lose 10% per day late. Graybox projects cannot be submitted late because they require in-class playtesting on specific dates. Arrange alternatives at least one week in advance.
course_policies.collaboration: Playtesting requires partnering with classmates, but all design work and grayboxing must be individual. You may not use another student's graybox layout as a starting point for your own work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Level designs must be original work. Recreating published game levels is acceptable only for analysis exercises, not for graded design submissions. All reference material and inspiration sources must be cited.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students must have Unreal Engine 5.3 installed with at least 50 GB of free disk space. A mouse with at least two buttons is required for viewport navigation. Lab machines are available for students whose hardware cannot run UE5.
